sanskrit_devanagari:
ध्यायतो विषयान्पुंसः सङ्गस्तेषूपजायते ।
सङ्गात्सञ्जायते कामः कामात्क्रोधोऽभिजायते ॥ ६२ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
dhyāyato viṣayān puṁsaḥ saṅgas teṣūpajāyate |
saṅgāt sañjāyate kāmaḥ kāmāt krodho 'bhijāyate || 62 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape, body text byte-identical to canonical)
exact (text body identical; uses single danda | as terminator)
exact (text body identical; verse-number marker rendered as ।।2.62।। rather than ॥ ६२ ॥)
IAST exact (Devanagari rendered without transliteration marks; IAST body matches: 'dhyayato visayan pumsah sangas tesu upajayate sangat sanjayate kamah kamat krodho bhijayate')

exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_02.062 = MBh_06,024.062). Body text identical: 'dhyāyato viṣayān puṃsaḥ saṅgas teṣūpajāyate / saṅgāt saṃjāyate kāmaḥ kāmāt krodho bhijāyate'. GRETIL writes 'puṃsaḥ' and 'saṃjāyate' with anusvara where vedabase prints 'puṁsaḥ' and 'sañjāyate' (candrabindu / palatal nasal); same Sanskrit conjuncts under different scholarly conventions.

anvaya.meaning: of one brooding / contemplating / dwelling on

translations.verbatim_quote: While contemplating the objects of the senses, a person develops attachment for them, and from such attachment lust develops, and from lust anger arises.
While contemplating on the objects of the senses, one develops attachment to them. Attachment leads to desire, and from desire arises anger.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: One who is not Kṛṣṇa conscious is subjected to material desires while contemplating the objects of the senses. The senses require real engagements, and if they are not engaged in the transcendental loving service of the Lord, they will certainly seek engagement in the service of materialism.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (short excerpt; Shankara comments on 2.62-2.63 together as one chained argument)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Pumsah, in the case of a person; dhyayatah, who dwells on, thinks of; visayan, the objects, the specialities of the objects such as sound etc.; upajayate, there arises; sangah, attachment, fondness, love; tesu, for them, for those objects. Sangat, from attachment, from love; sanjayate, grows; kamah, hankering, thirst. When that is obstructed from any quarter, kamat, from hankering; abhijayate, springs; krodhah, anger.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads 2.62-2.63 as one chained argument and his commentary often treats them together. The cascade begins with dhyāna on viṣayas — brooding contemplation of sense-objects, attending to their specialities (their attractions). From such brooding arises saṅga (attachment). Saṅga, when intensified, becomes kāma (active desire). When kāma is obstructed by any quarter — by another agent, by reality, by the absence of the desired object — krodha (anger) springs up. Each transition is distinct and each requires a precondition: brooding precedes attachment, attachment precedes desire, obstructed desire precedes anger.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (short excerpt of translator-rendered Ramanuja bhashya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Indeed, in respect of a person, whose attachment to sense-objects is expelled but whose mind is not focussed on Me, even though he controls the senses, contemplation on sense-objects is unavoidable on account of the impressions of sins from time immemorial. Again attachment increases fully in 'a man who thinks about sense-objects'. From attachment arises desire. What is called 'desire' is the further stage of attachment.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja diagnoses the cascade more sharply: even a practitioner who has expelled active attachment but has not fixed the mind on the Supreme cannot avoid contemplation of sense-objects, because the saṁskāras (impressions from past actions) keep returning the mind to those objects. From returning contemplation, attachment increases; from intensified attachment, desire (kāma) arises as the next stage; from obstructed desire, anger arises. Ramanuja thus links 2.62's cascade directly to 2.61's prescription: only the focus on the Supreme breaks the loop, because only that focus replaces the saṁskāra-driven contemplation of viṣayas with contemplation of the auspicious object.

positions.rendering: who dwells on, thinks of (the specialities of the objects)
thinks about (driven by saṁskāras when the mind is not focussed on the Supreme)

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: dhyāyataḥ is a present participle of dhyai- (root: meditate, brood, dwell on). All four readings agree on the lexical sense ('contemplating, brooding'), but Shankara emphasises that the contemplation is on the 'specialities' (charms) of the objects — not just neutral perception but attentive dwelling on what is attractive. Ramanuja contextualises: this contemplation is structurally unavoidable for someone whose mind is not yet focussed on the Supreme, because the saṁskāras drive it. The verb is not 'briefly notices' but 'dwells on' — broods. The cascade does not begin with mere perception; it begins with the operator's repeated returning of attention to the object.

positions.rendering: attachment → desire → anger (in that strict sequence)
explicitly preserves the sequence as three distinct transitions: brooding → attachment → desire (when intensified) → anger (when obstructed)

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: There is no translator disagreement here, but the verse's cascade structure is so important to the next verse (2.63) that the sequence is logged for transparency. Each transition is distinct: saṅga is settled fondness toward the object; kāma is active hankering, the further stage of saṅga; krodha is the response to obstruction of kāma. The verse names all three transitions precisely; the cascade in 2.63 continues from krodha through four more steps. Together 2.62-2.63 give an eight-step psychological analysis, and each step is preserved as a distinct transition by all major commentators.

literal_meaning: Of a person brooding on sense-objects, attachment to them is born; from attachment, desire is born; from desire, anger arises.
traditional_meaning_consensus: This verse is the first half of the Gita's most precise psychological cascade — together with 2.63 it forms an eight-step analysis of how the operator falls. The cascade is sequential and cumulative: each step requires the previous step as precondition, and each step intensifies into the next. Step 1: dhyāna on viṣayas (brooding contemplation of sense-objects, dwelling on their attractive specialities). Step 2: saṅga (attachment, settled fondness). Step 3: kāma (active desire, the further stage of attachment). Step 4: krodha (anger, when desire is obstructed). Shankara reads the verse alongside 2.63 as one chained argument; Ramanuja diagnoses the structural cause (saṁskāra-driven contemplation when the mind is not focussed on the Supreme); Gaudiya Vaishnava reading folds the prescription back to Krishna-consciousness as the only way to break the loop. All traditions agree the cascade is real, the sequence is fixed, and prevention is upstream — at the dhyāna stage, before saṅga has formed.

remaining_gaps: Shankara's full Sanskrit bhashya not pulled — only translator-rendered (Gambhirananda) excerpt was machine-extractable; Shankara historically combines 2.62-2.63 as one chained argument, which the excerpt preserves.
